content.full_text:
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Lance Olson 
Attorney at Law 
926 J Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Mr. Olson: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
August 18, 1983 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice, Our 
Advice No. A-83-187 
Thank you for your telephone request for advice on behalf of 
Assemblyman Lloyd G. Connelly and Assemblyman Phillip Isenberg. 
Both of the Assemblymen are in the process of terminating their 
partnerships in their respective law firms. 
After the ter-
mination, they may continue to perform work for their former 
firms under various financial arrangements. 
You have asked how 
the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act 
will apply to two of the proposed financial arrangements. 
These 
arrangements are as follows: 
1. 
The Assemblymen will be paid a retainer to be available 
to consult on cases that are being primarily handled by other 
attorneys in their former firms. 
If the Assemblymen perform any 
work on the cases, they w~ll be paid, on an hourly basis, from 
the firm's general fund. 
2. 
The Assemblymen will work on their former firm's 
contingency fee cases and they will be paid a percentage of the 
amount of the recovery. 
Government Code Section 871001/ provides that no state 
public official can make or participate in the making of a 
governmental decision if he knows or has reason to know he has a 
financial interest in the decision. 
An official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 
1/ All statutory references made are to the Government 
Code, unless otherwise stated. 


Lance Olson 
August 18, 1983 
Page 2 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will 
have a material financial effect on: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth over 
$1,000. 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income ••• aggregating $250 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or promised to 
the public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made •••• 
Section 87103. 
In the present case, the Assemblymen are divesting 
themselves of their investment interests in their firms. 
with respect to their law firms or their firms' clients, 
will only need to determine whether their decisions will 
material financial effect on a source of income. 
Thus, 
they 
have a 
Under both financial arrangements, the Assemblymen will have 
to disqualify themselves from a decision if it is foreseeable 
that it will have a direct effect on their income.~ In 
addition, under the financial arrangement in which the 
Assemblymen are paid, on an hourly basis, for work they perform 
on other attorneys' cases, their respective law firms will be 
sources of income to them and they may have to disqualify 
themselves on decisions affecting the firms. 
Under the contingency fee arrangement, the determination as 
to whether the law firm or the client is the source of income 
depends upon the facts of the particular situation and 
Assemblymen Connelly or Isenberg should contact us for advice at 
the time that they are actually confronted with this situation. 
Because the Assemblymen will no longer have 10% or greater 
investment interests in their firms, the clients of the firms' 
other attorneys will not be sources of income to them, barring 
the Assemblymen's involvement in the manner described above. 
~ See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (b) (3) (A) • 


Lance Olson 
August 18, 1983 
Page 3 
Thank you very much for contacting us on this matter. 
If I 
can be of any further help to you, please feel free to contact 
me at 916/322-5901. 
Very truly yours, 
~a§~!11Z~ 
Counsel, Legal Division 
JSM: km

sections.question: How will the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to two proposed financial arrangements for Assemblymen Lloyd G. Connelly and Phillip Isenberg as they terminate their partnerships in their respective law firms? Specifically, how will the Act apply to: (1) a retainer arrangement where the Assemblymen will be available to consult on cases primarily handled by other attorneys and paid hourly for any work performed, and (2) a contingency fee arrangement where the Assemblymen will work on their former firm's contingency fee cases and receive a percentage of recovery?
sections.conclusion: Under both financial arrangements, the Assemblymen will have to disqualify themselves from a decision if it is foreseeable that it will have a direct effect on their income. In addition, under the hourly-based arrangement, their respective law firms will be sources of income to them and they may have to disqualify themselves on decisions affecting the firms. Under the contingency fee arrangement, whether the law firm or client is the source of income depends on the facts of the particular situation and the Assemblymen should contact FPPC for advice when actually confronted with such a situation. Because the Assemblymen will no longer have 10% or greater investment interests in their firms, the clients of the firms' other attorneys will not be sources of income to them, barring their involvement as described.
sections.question_synthetic: How do the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions apply to two proposed financial arrangements under which two Assemblymen would continue performing legal work for their former law firms after terminating their partnerships?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Assemblymen must disqualify themselves from decisions with foreseeable direct effects on their income from either arrangement. Under the hourly-based arrangement, they may also need to disqualify themselves from decisions affecting their former law firms. Under the contingency fee arrangement, they should seek additional advice when actually facing specific situations.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document follows standard FPPC advice letter format with clear question and conclusion sections. Statutory references to Government Code Section 87100 and 87103 provided. Minor extraction confidence deduction due to lack of explicit 'QUESTION' and 'CONCLUSION' section headers, though content is clearly identifiable.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: How will the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act apply to two proposed financial arrangements for Assemblymen Lloyd G. Connelly and Phillip Isenberg as they terminate their partnerships in their respective law firms? Specifically, how will the Act apply to: (1) a retainer arrangement where the Assemblymen will be available to consult on cases primarily handled by other attorneys and paid hourly for any work performed, and (2) a contingency fee arrangement where the Assemblymen will work on their former firm's contingency fee cases and receive a percentage of recovery?

CONCLUSION: Under both financial arrangements, the Assemblymen will have to disqualify themselves from a decision if it is foreseeable that it will have a direct effect on their income. In addition, under the hourly-based arrangement, their respective law firms will be sources of income to them and they may have to disqualify themselves on decisions affecting the firms. Under the contingency fee arrangement, whether the law firm or client is the source of income depends on the facts of the particular situation and the Assemblymen should contact FPPC for advice when actually confronted with such a situation. Because the Assemblymen will no longer have 10% or greater investment interests in their firms, the clients of the firms' other attorneys will not be sources of income to them, barring their involvement as described.

embedding.summary: The FPPC provides advice to Assemblymen Lloyd G. Connelly and Phillip Isenberg regarding conflict of interest rules applicable to two proposed financial arrangements for continued legal work with their former law firms after terminating their partnerships.
